data.title: Nvidia Chip Restriction Much More Severe than BeforeMarket Talk
data.description: New restrictions on selling semiconductors to China could significantly reduce the market opportunity for American chip companies, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells WSJ. "While the extent of the restrictions will only be clear once the U.S. publishes the details, Nvidia's disclosure indicates the restrictions are much more severe than they were before," Luria says. Nvidia slides 9%, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
Market Talks covering the impact of U.S. Politics and White House policies on companies and markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1409 ET - New restrictions on selling semiconductors to China could significantly reduce the market opportunity for American chip companies, DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria tells WSJ. "While the extent of the restrictions will only be clear once the U.S. publishes the details, Nvidia's disclosure indicates the restrictions are much more severe than they were before," Luria says. Nvidia slides 9%, and is the worst performer in the S&P 500. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1353 ET - If the goal of U.S. government restrictions on Nvidia's sales of H20 chips in China was to restrict Beijing's AI capabilities, that ship has already sailed, BofA Securities analyst Vivek Arya tells WSJ. "I understand the goal is to restrict AI, but limiting this product is no way limiting China's AI capability because Huawei Technologies can make products that have similar capabilities," Arya says. The risk is that if Huawei takes its products and is able to sell them into other emerging markets and other places outside of China at a very low price, there is nothing to prevent them from doing that. And it wouldn't help the trade gap either, he adds. "It's hard to defend these moves." Nvidia slides 8%. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1339 ET - The strength in March U.S. retail sales isn't only about consumers trying to get ahead of tariffs, Wells Fargo's Tim Quinlan and Shannon Grein write. Sales beat WSJ consensus in both the headline and the ex-auto figures. The report included upward revisions of previous data. Autos saw the biggest March gain, likely reflecting a rush to buy vehicles before any price increase due to tariffs. But consumers "also spent more at restaurants in March, and we're not aware of a way you can front-run tariffs with a nice night out," Quinlan and Grein say. "How consumers act in these next couple of months will be telling," they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1320 ET - Prologis customers are accelerating shipments, rerouting volume and have urgent demand for overflow space as they try to manage tariff volatility, says Tim Arndt, CFO of the biggest industrial REIT, in an analyst call. Free trade zones and bonded warehouses also are being evaluated. Prologis sees inventory levels rising as businesses stockpile and build resiliency, and global markets in its portfolio such as Canada, India and Brazil will become more important. Mexico stands out in terms of growing interest before and after April's tariff announcement, he says. (mark.long@wsj.com)

1310 ET - The government of Canada's most populous province is moving to drop barriers to trade and labor mobility in the country with the introduction of a "Protect Ontario Through Free Trade Within Canada Act." Premier Doug Ford says the economy has long been held back by red tape and regulations, making Canada vulnerable to external threats, including from President Trump's tariffs. The move is part of a wave of support federally and provincially for easing trade within Canada to help counter the hit from Trump's trade policies. Ontario's act wold allow workers from other parts of the country to more easily work in the province, allow more regulated health professions to begin practicing in Ontario while waiting for registration, allow direct-to-consumer alcohol sales with reciprocating provinces, and remove all party-specific exceptions under the Canadian Free Trade Agreement. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1306 ET - Fitch Ratings says it lowered its forecasts for 2025 world growth by 0.4 percentage points, given the escalation in the global trade war. "World growth is projected to fall below 2% this year, which would be the weakest since 2009 excluding the pandemic," according to Fitch. Growth in the U.S. and in China has been cut by 0.5 pp, according to Fitch. Annual growth in the U.S. is expected at 1.2%, but will slow through 2025 to register just 0.4% year-over-year in 4Q25, Fitch says. In China, growth is expected to fall below 4% this year and in 2026. In the euro zone, growth expectations are stuck below 1%, Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1211 ET - As with the March decision, Bank of Canada senior officials debated whether to cut rates again, or hold their fire while awaiting more information. In the end, there was "clear consensus" on keeping the policy rate unchanged after a "really good deliberations" among top policymakers, says BOC's No. 2 official, Carolyn Rogers. She tells reporters there were differing opinions among the seven-member governing council on how big a hit there would be to the economy from US tariffs. Some officials, she says, were "more optimistic that the effects won't be really big." She adds some members suggested "you won't see that really big upward pressure on inflation." More details on the deliberations will emerge in two weeks when the BOC releases minutes of the governing council's discussions. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1149 ET--The timeline shared by Nvidia Tuesday afternoon regarding Trump's imposed China-related restrictions for its H20 chips explains the rushed announcement that the graphic chip maker would build $500 billion of AI infrastructure over the next four years, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. The appeal to the Trump administration was aimed at mitigating the final regulation, the analysts say. "Nvidia may now choose not to follow through on this pledge given the outcome, and if it does, we believe it will erode long-term margins," the analysts add. Nvidia shares fall 6.4% to $105.03. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1129 ET--Nvidia's $5.5 billion charge related to Trump's export restrictions to its H20 chips won't only have a significant impact on the company's first-quarter gross margins, but also its revenue growth during the entire fiscal year, William Blair analysts say in a research note. "While we still believe that Nvidia is the leader in AI infrastructure, with a significant technology moat and robust demand for its next-generation BlackWell GPUs, this will only add to the wall of worry that has built up around the name over the last few months," the analysts say. Shares may be range-bound in the near term as macro uncertainty persists, the analysts say. However, they urge investors "not to miss the forest for the trees" when it comes to a secular winner of the AI platform shift. Shares fall 6.8% to $104.61. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1114 ET - U.S. and eurozone credit assets are still attractive, given the healthy state of corporate balance sheets, manageable debt levels, and availability of cash chasing attractive yields, Societe Generale's Juan Valencia says in a note. "We expect the different credit indices to extend the recent tightening trend," he says. There is a risk, however, that trade tensions drive economies into recession which could hurt credit markets, Societe Generale says. "The risk of a recession in the EU is small (higher and rising in the U.S.), and unless it is a long or deep recession, we believe that spreads will not react as badly as in previous crises." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1111 ET - While the Bank of Canada held interest rates steady there is scope for officials to be debating whether they need to lower the policy rate a half percentage point at the next scheduled meeting in June should trade tensions with the U.S. continue to de-escalate, Desjardins' Royce Mendes suggests. He says that doesn't open up the possibility of inter-meeting cuts, just that trade wars tend to be slower moving assuming financial markets remain orderly. Mendes continues to forecast the policy rate will be cut to a low of 1.75% later this year. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1109 ET - Uncertainty about the path for U.S. trade policy and tariffs and how that fall out for Canada's economy and inflation there has kept the Bank of Canada on the sidelines. Oxford Economics reckons that while a couple more quarter percentage point cuts to the policy rate from the current 2.75% can't be entirely ruled out, it doesn't expect the central bank will lower below 2.25%--the low end of its neutral range--at least until it gets more clarity on the path for trade and fiscal policy. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Sector Update: Financial Stocks Decline in Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Financial stocks were lower in Wednesday afternoon trading, with the NYSE Financial Index down 1.1% and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLF) dropping 1.7%.

The Philadelphia Housing Index was falling 1.6%, while the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) was up 0.4%.

Bitcoin (BTC/USD) was down 1% to $83,639, and the yield for 10-year US Treasuries was 4.2 basis points lower at 4.28%.

In economic news, the U.S. Census Bureau reported retail sales rose 1.4% in March from the prior month, compared with a 0.2% gain in February and ahead of the FactSet consensus estimate for an increase of 1.3%.

Global trade volume is expected to decline this year as new tariffs fuel uncertainty, with "severe" downside risks that could deal a bigger blow to cross-border imports and exports, the World Trade Organization said Wednesday. Trade volume across the world is expected to decrease 0.2% in 2025 followed by growth of 2.5% in 2026, according to the WTO's latest global trade outlook and statistics report. Trade increased 2.9% in 2024.

In corporate news, KKR is mulling a potential sale of Atlantic Aviation that may value the private jet fixed base operator at $10 billion, Bloomberg reported. KKR shares were down 3%.

US Bancorp reported better-than-expected Q1 results on Wednesday, while the lender reiterated its full-year revenue growth outlook. Its shares still fell 1.8%.

T. Rowe Price Investment Management plans to support activist Starboard Value's director nominees for the board of Autodesk (ADSK), Reuters reported. T. Rowe shares were down 2.5%.

data.title: Farmers delay sales while waiting for rain
data.description:
Spot basis bids for hard red winter (HRW) wheat remained mostly steady in the southern U.S. Plains on Wednesday as farmer selling was minimal, dealers said.

* Farmers are holding off on booking advance sales of the upcoming harvest because of uncertainty over whether rains will arrive to ease harmful dryness, dealers said.

* Warm, dry conditions were further reducing soil moisture in the Plains on Wednesday, according to a daily U.S. Department of Agriculture weather report. Rains are expected this weekend in the region.

* K.C. May hard red winter wheat futures (KWK25) were up 6 cents at $5.59 a bushel by 12:55 p.m. CDT (1755 GMT).

data.title: Luminar Forms Advisory Council to Support Growth Strategy
data.description:
Luminar Technologies said Wednesday that it established an advisory council, Luminar Automotive Council, to help with its growth strategy.

For the council, the company said it has tapped former executives of various automakers including Mercedes-Benz, Tesla , Jaguar Land Rover and Ford Motor , as well as the US Department of Transportation.

The automotive technology company said it is focused on enabling adoption at scale of its light detection and ranging technology, Luminar Halo. The council will help in establishing relationships with potential customers, original equipment manufacturers, contract manufacturing partners and other ecosystem players in facilitating this adoption, Luminar said.

The council members will also advise the company on major industry trends, overall market conditions and the regulatory environment, the company said.

data.title: Volkswagen to keep vehicle prices steady through May
data.description:
Volkswagen confirmed Wednesday that it is joining the growing group of automakers committing to keeping prices steady to ease car shoppers' concerns around tariffs.

Automakers have provided stability, and even deep discounts, to consumers amid worries that tariffs will drive up prices on dealer lots. In a flurry of announcements earlier this month, U.S. and foreign-owned carmakers offered a variety of deals or price assurances, looking to capitalize on a surge of nervous shoppers hoping to clinch a deal.

German carmaker Volkswagen confirmed it is holding prices steady through May, a similar move to Hyundai Motor , which committed to maintaining sticker prices on its current models through June 2.

Ford Motor and Stellantis earlier this month offered shoppers deep discounts across their lineups, and Nissan reduced prices on its 2025 Rogue and 2025 Pathfinder.

While the short-term buying frenzy is a boon to carmakers and their dealers, auto executives have warned that sustained tariffs will add excessive costs to the industry, and ultimately weigh on consumers.

data.title: FED'S POWELL Q&A/ECC: 'ABSOLUTELY' READY TO SUPPLY SWAP LINE DOLLAR FUNDING FOR OTHER CENTRAL BANKS IF NEEDED
Are Foreign Investors Selling U.S. Assets? A Treasury Department Report Will Offer Clues.
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

Every month, the Treasury Department publishes past data on U.S. assets sold and bought by foreign central banks and investors. No ones typically sweats the numbers — but as President Donald Trump's trade policies continue to roil markets, investors must pay attention to the latest report.

The Treasury is set to release its Treasury International Capital data for February at 4 p.m. Eastern on Wednesday, an official confirmed with Barron's.

The report comes as fears about foreigners punishing Donald Trump's trade policies by selling U.S. assets has gripped financial markets. Stocks saw big swings last week, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. government bonds surged to 4.492% on Friday — its highest level since mid-February.

Higher yields mean someone, somewhere, is selling bonds.

The Treasury data can offer some clarity on foreign selling. Investors should pay special attention to the monthly data on transactions of long-term securities, which have a maturity that's more than one year old. This figure will show whether foreigners have been net sellers or buyers of U.S. Treasuries, corporate bonds, stocks, and other assets.

The report only shows market data from six weeks ago. However, given Trump started announcing global tariffs in February, the report could signal if foreigner central bankers and investors had a knee-jerk reaction to tariffs.

Treasury data so far show investors sold longer-term Treasuries for the three prior consecutive months. In January, foreigners sold a net $13.3 billion of U.S. notes and bonds, $49.69 billion in December, and $34.41 billion in November amid the U.S. election cycle.

Japan is the largest foreign holder of Treasuries; its holdings are down about $250 billion since late 2021. China, meanwhile, is the second-largest holder — and its holdings are at their lowest level since 2009.

Despite geopolitical pressures and past data, no one can say definitively that foreign nations are using their U.S. debt holdings as weapons of financial warfare. China's main state institutions own at least $3 trillion in dollar assets — and deliberately causing a selloff would be self-harming, wrote Mark Williams, Chief Asia Economist at Capital Economics on Monday.

Not only is it in China and other foreign countries' best interest to maintain the value of their U.S. holdings, they also have fewer options. The U.S. Treasury market is by far the safest and the largest in the world. Some of the U.S. companies selling stocks have market capitalizations that are more than a trillion dollars — surpassing the gross domestic product of many emerging nations.

"The only reason a fire sale could make financial sense would be if Chinese officials thought that the US government was about to freeze its assets or default on Chinese-owned bonds," Williams added.

It would be overkill to predict the U.S. will default on its debt, a catastrophic situation that's never occurred — and would almost certainly lead to a recession. However, the idea that the U.S. could freeze assets of foreign nations has heightened after Russia's U.S. funds were frozen in 2022, following the invasion of Ukraine.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: April Homebuilder Confidence Surprisingly Edges Up
data.description:
US homebuilder confidence unexpectedly picked up in April, though sentiment remained subdued amid heightened economic uncertainty, according to National Association of Home Builders and Wells Fargo data released Wednesday.

The housing market index, which covers new single-family homes, edged higher by one point sequentially to 40 this month. The consensus was for the index to drop to 38, according to a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The March index level of 39 was the lowest in seven months.

"The recent dip in mortgage rates may have pushed some buyers off the fence in March, helping builders with sales activity," NAHB Chairman Buddy Hughes said. "At the same time, builders have expressed growing uncertainty over market conditions as tariffs have increased price volatility for building materials at a time when the industry continues to grapple with labor shortages and a lack of buildable lots."

After announcing sweeping new tariffs on US imports earlier this month, President Donald Trump declared a 90-day pause on certain tariffs for non-retaliating countries. China responded with its own retaliatory duties, and Washington and Beijing have since raised tariffs on each other's goods multiple times.

"Policy uncertainty is having a negative impact on home builders, making it difficult for them to accurately price homes and make critical business decisions," said NAHB Chief Economist Robert Dietz. "The April HMI data indicates that the tariff cost effect is already taking hold, with the majority of builders reporting cost increases on building materials due to tariffs."

Homebuilders' suppliers have increased prices by an average 6.3% due to tariffs, the NAHB survey showed.

The index gauging current sales conditions rose to 45 this month from 43 in March, which was the lowest since December 2023. The metric charting expectations over the next six months dropped four points to 43, the NAHB survey showed.

data.title: Kuehn Law Encourages Investors of Five Below, Inc. to Contact Law Firm
data.description:
NEW YORK, April 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Kuehn Law, PLLC, a shareholder litigation law firm, is investigating whether certain officers and directors of Five Below, Inc. breached their fiduciary duties to shareholders.

According to a federal securities lawsuit, Five Below insiders caused the company to misrepresent or fail to disclose the true nature of the company’s financial strength and operations, including its outlook for the first quarter and full year 2024. On June 5, 2024, FIVE announced disappointing first quarter 2024 sales result and cut its full year 2024 guidance stating, "Net sales are expected to be in the range of $3.79 billion to $3.87 billion based on opening approximately 230 new stores." At the same time, FIVE claimed that for the second quarter, "Net sales are expected to be in the range of $830 million to $850 million based on opening approximately 60 new stores."

If you currently own FIVE and purchased prior to December 1, 2022 please contact Justin Kuehn, Esq. here, by email at justin@kuehn.law or call (833) 672-0814.  The consultation and case are free with no obligation to you. Kuehn Law pays all case costs and does not charge its investor clients. Shareholders should contact the firm immediately as there may be limited time to enforce your rights.  

Why Your Participation Matters:

As a shareholder your voice matters, and by getting involved, you contribute to the integrity and fairness of the financial markets. Your investment. Your voice. Your future.™  

For additional information, please visit Shareholder Derivative Litigation - Kuehn Law.

Attorney advertising. Prior results do not guarantee similar outcomes.

data.title: Montero Mining Announces Share Consolidation, $15-Million Return of Capital
data.description:
Montero Mining and Exploration on Wednesday said it is consolidating its shares and returning capital to shareholders as it finalizes its uses of the proceeds from the settlement with the Tanzania government over the expropriation of the Wigu Hill rare earth element project.

The company will consolidate its shares on a six-for-one basis. The shares will trade on post-consolidation basis on May 5.

Montero is also seeking shareholder approval to distribute $15 million to shareholders consisting of a cash distribution of $0.30 per pre-consolidation share or $1.80 on a post-consolidation basis.

"After careful evaluation of the alternatives, we believe this is the appropriate course of action to deliver immediate value to shareholders who have supported Montero through some very challenging times. The return of capital provides shareholders with meaningful cash compensation for the expropriation of the Wigu Hill Rare Earth Project in Tanzania," Chief Executive Tony Harwood said. "Montero will retain approximately C$3.4 million in cash where further exploration is planned at our Avispa copper gold project and the company evaluates new opportunities."

Shareholders will be asked to approve the distribution at a special meeting to be held on June 11.

The company's shares were last seen down $0.01 to $0.31 on the TSX Venture Exchange.

data.title: Ghana government takes control of Gold Fields' Damang mine, mines ministry says
data.description:
Ghana's government has assumed operational control of Gold Fields' Damang mine after it rejected an application from the South African company to renew its lease, the mines ministry said on Wednesday.

The Johannesburg-based miner said on Monday that it had unsuccessfully applied to extend the Damang lease, which is due to expire on April 18, and was winding down operations as a result.

It was only processing stockpiles at Damang after ceasing mining operations in 2023, although it had committed to exit the operation in an orderly way as part of its end-of-life plan.

"The Damang Mine's return to state oversight marks a critical step in Ghana's economic reset, ensuring its gold reserves directly benefit citizens," the mines ministry said in a statement.

It said the decision aligned with policy moves to stop the "neo-colonial" automatic renewal of mining licenses in gold-producing Ghana, but reassessing in order to maximise national benefit.

"We are on the lookout for value propositions on the utilisation of our mineral resources that align with the same," the ministry added.

Damang is the smaller of Gold Fields' two mines in Ghana after Tarkwa, the biggest open pit gold mine in the country. Damang produced 135,000 ounces of gold in 2024, about 6% of the group's total output of 2.15 million ounces.

The company has been weighing selling its smaller operations - including Damang and the Cerro Corona mine in Peru, which has less than five years left on its lifespan - as it focuses on its newly commissioned Salares Norte mine in Chile and the Windfall project in Canada.

data.title: PREVIEW: American Express EPS, revenue seen rising in Q1
data.description:
** American Express shares down 2.3% to $251.82 ahead of Q1 results due before the opening bell on Thursday

** The global payments company is expected to report EPS of $3.47 on revenue of $16.94 bln, per LSEG data, vs yr-ago results of $3.33 in EPS on revenue of $15.80 bln

** AXP has met or beaten EPS view in four straight qtrs; on revenue side, most recent miss was Q3 2024

** Of the 31 analysts that cover American Express Co, the breakdown of recommendations is 12 "strong buy" or "buy," 17 "hold" and 2 "sell" or "strong sell" ratings

** Median price target of $282.50 is down from $316.40 a month ago

** AXP shares down nearly 15% YTD vs >6% drop in Dow Industrials

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Fall in Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Consumer stocks were lower Wednesday afternoon, with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) shedding 0.4% and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) dropping 1.8%.

In corporate news, Tesla has paused the shipping of key components from China for its upcoming Cybercab and Semi models after significant increases to US tariffs on China, Reuters reported. Separately, Tesla car registrations in California in Q1 fell 15% from a year ago, the California New Car Dealers Association said. Tesla shares were down more than 5%.

United Airlines swung to a higher-than-expected Q1 profit, but the air carrier warned of a potential negative impact on its full-year earnings if the US economy enters a recession. Shares were down 2.1%.

Harley-Davidson activist investor H Partners is preparing for a proxy battle to remove three directors, including chief executive officer Jochen Zeitz, following a sharp decline in sales and a plummeting stock price, the investment firm said in a statement. Harley-Davidson shares rose 0.7%.

data.title: Top Midday Gainers
data.description:
RadNet has agreed to acquire iCad in an all-stock deal valued at roughly $103 million, they said late Tuesday.

Shares of iCad soared 71%, with intraday trading volume of over 17.2 million from a daily average of about 508,000.

Treasure Global struck a partnership with Mezzofy (Hong Kong), securing distribution rights for Mezzofy's digital coupon platform in Malaysia. It expects to generate $2 million to $4 million in annual revenue in the first 12 months of the rollout.

Shares surged 57% as intraday trading volume soared to more than 92 million from a daily average of about 354,000.

Mira Pharmaceuticals said Wednesday that data demonstrated the potential efficacy of its oral ketamine analog, Ketamir-2, in treating diabetic neuropathy in an animal model.

Shares advanced 15%, with intraday trading volume rising to over 44.8 million from a daily average of about 255,000.

data.title: Sector Update: Energy Stocks Gain Wednesday Afternoon
data.description:
Energy stocks advanced Wednesday afternoon with the NYSE Energy Sector Index rising 1.8% and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) adding 1.1%.

The Philadelphia Oil Service Sector Index rose 0.8%, and the Dow Jones US Utilities Index eased 0.2%.

US crude oil stocks, including those in the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, rose by 800,000 barrels in the week ended Friday following an increase of 2.8 million in the previous week. Excluding inventories in the SPR, commercial crude oil stocks rose by 500,000 barrels after a gain of 2.6 million in the previous week, compared with the increase of 945,000 expected in a survey compiled by Bloomberg.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil climbed 1.7% to $62.37 a barrel, and global benchmark Brent advanced 1.7% to $65.79 a barrel. Henry Hub natural gas futures fell 2.9% to $3.23 per 1 million BTU.

In corporate news, Phillips 66 shareholder Elliott Investment Management said findings from its March survey of the oil company's institutional investors showed they "share Elliott's frustration" with the "underperformance and lack of a compelling strategic direction." Phillips 66 shares rose 0.7%.

Woodside Energy is in talks with Uniper on the potential supply of liquefied natural gas. Woodside shares rose 0.1%.

Sunnova Energy International faces difficulties securing fresh capital from creditors to avoid bankruptcy, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter. Sunnova shares dropped 17%.

data.title: Venture Global, Inc. Announces Timing of First Quarter 2025 Earnings Release and Conference Call
data.description:
ARLINGTON, Va.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--April 16, 2025--

Venture Global, Inc. ("Venture Global") announced today that it plans to issue its earnings release with respect to first quarter 2025 financial results before market open on Tuesday, May 13, 2025.

Venture Global will host a conference call for investors and analysts beginning at 9:00 am Eastern Time (ET) on May 13, 2025, to discuss first quarter results.

A listen-only webcast of the conference call and accompanying slide presentation will be available at Venture Global's Investor Relations website HERE. After the conclusion of the conference call, a replay will be made available on the Venture Global website.

About Venture Global

Venture Global is a long-term, low-cost provider of U.S. LNG sourced from resource rich North American natural gas basins. Venture Global's business includes assets across the LNG supply chain including LNG production, natural gas transport, shipping and regasification. Venture Global's first facility, Calcasieu Pass, commenced producing LNG in January 2022. The company's second facility, Plaquemines LNG, achieved first production of LNG in December 2024. The company is currently constructing and developing over 100 MTPA of nameplate production capacity to provide clean, affordable energy to the world. Venture Global is developing Carbon Capture and Sequestration projects at each of its LNG facilities.

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20250416356923/en/

Sherwin-Williams Shareholders Elect New Director
data.description:
Robert J. Gamgort, Executive Chairman of Keurig Dr Pepper Inc., joins the Board

CLEVELAND, April 16, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — The Sherwin-Williams Company announced that Robert J. Gamgort was elected to the Board of Directors of Sherwin-Williams at the company's Annual Meeting of Shareholders today. Following the Annual Meeting, Sherwin-Williams' Board now consists of nine members, including eight independent directors. Mr. Gamgort was also appointed to the Audit Committee of the Board.

Mr. Gamgort, 62, currently serves as Executive Chairman of Keurig Dr Pepper Inc. , a leading beverage company in North America.

"We are thrilled to welcome Bob to our Board of Directors," said Heidi G. Petz, Chair, President and Chief Executive Officer of Sherwin-Williams. "His depth of leadership experience in the consumer products industry will enable him to engage on a variety of matters relevant to Sherwin-Williams' relentless focus on customer success, overall business strategy, and global operations. We look forward to his contributions to the Board, our Company, and our employees, customers, shareholders and other stakeholders in the years to come."

Mr. Gamgort has served as Executive Chairman of the Board of Directors of Keurig Dr Pepper since 2022, and prior to that, as Chairman since 2019. He also served as President and Chief Executive Officer of Keurig Dr Pepper from the company's inception in 2018 until April 2024. Prior to the formation of Keurig Dr Pepper, Mr. Gamgort was the Chief Executive Officer of Keurig Green Mountain, a role he assumed in 2016. Mr. Gamgort also served as the Chief Executive Officer of Pinnacle Foods from 2009 to 2016, and he held various leadership roles at Mars, Inc. from 1998 to 2009, and Kraft Foods from 1986 to 1997.

Mr. Gamgort received a bachelor's degree in economics from Bucknell University and an M.B.A. from the Kellogg Graduate School of Management at Northwestern University.

About The Sherwin-Williams Company

Founded in 1866, The Sherwin-Williams Company is a global leader in the manufacture, development, distribution, and sale of paint, coatings and related products to professional, industrial, commercial, and retail customers. The Company manufactures products under well-known brands such as Sherwin-Williams(R), Valspar(R), HGTV HOME(R) by Sherwin-Williams, Dutch Boy(R), Krylon(R), Minwax(R), Thompson's(R) WaterSeal(R), Cabot(R) and many more. With global headquarters in Cleveland, Ohio, Sherwin-Williams(R) branded products are sold exclusively through a chain of more than 5,000 Company-operated stores and branches, while the Company's other brands are sold through leading mass merchandisers, home centers, independent paint dealers, hardware stores, automotive retailers, and industrial distributors. The Sherwin-Williams Performance Coatings Group supplies a broad range of highly-engineered solutions for the construction, industrial, packaging and transportation markets in more than 120 countries around the world. Sherwin-Williams shares are traded on the New York Stock Exchange (symbol: SHW). For more information, visit www.sherwin.com.
